concepts.title: Balanced incomplete block designs
concepts.blurb: A $(v, k, \lambda)$-BIBD is a collection of $k$-subsets (blocks) of a $v$-set so that every pair of points appears in exactly $\lambda$ blocks. Necessary conditions $\lambda(v-1) = r(k-1)$ and $bk = vr$ link the parameters $(v,b,r,k,\lambda)$ but are far from sufficient — Fisher's inequality and Bruck–Ryser–Chowla cut deeper.

concepts.title: Fisher's inequality
concepts.blurb: Any non-trivial $2$-design satisfies $b \ge v$: at least as many blocks as points. The proof inspects the incidence matrix $N$ and shows $NN^\top = (r-\lambda)I + \lambda J$ has full rank $v$, forcing $\mathrm{rank}(N) = v$, hence $b \ge v$.

concepts.title: Finite projective planes
concepts.blurb: A projective plane of order $q$ has $q^2 + q + 1$ points and the same number of lines, with $q+1$ points on each line — the symmetric $(q^2+q+1, q+1, 1)$-design. They exist iff $q$ is a prime power, with Bruck–Ryser–Chowla forbidding $q \equiv 1, 2 \pmod 4$ unless $q$ is a sum of two squares (ruling out e.g. $q=10$).

concepts.title: Latin squares & MOLS
concepts.blurb: A Latin square of order $n$ is an $n \times n$ array filled so each row and column is a permutation of $[n]$. Two are orthogonal if their superposition lists every pair $(a,b) \in [n]^2$ once. A complete set of $n-1$ MOLS exists iff a projective plane of order $n$ does — Euler's $36$-officers problem closed the $n=6$ case (no MOLS pair).

concepts.title: Designs from codes
concepts.blurb: Linear codes and combinatorial designs are deeply intertwined. The supports of minimum-weight codewords in the Hamming $[7,4,3]$ code form the Fano plane $\mathrm{PG}(2,2)$, the unique Steiner triple system $S(2,3,7)$. The binary Golay code $G_{23}$ yields the Steiner system $S(4,7,23)$, and perfect codes correspond to specific $t$-designs by Assmus–Mattson.

concepts.title: Applications: experiments, testing, geometry
concepts.blurb: BIBDs were born from agricultural statistics — Fisher used them to test $v$ fertilizers on $b$ field plots so every pair is compared with equal precision. Latin squares schedule pairwise-orthogonal interactions in software testing; finite affine planes enable threshold cryptography; Steiner systems underlie tournament round-robins.
